candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: Accelerating Parallel Sampling of Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for image
generation. However, sampling from diffusion models is usually time-consuming
due to the inherent autoregressive nature of their sampling process. In this
work, we propose a novel approach that accelerates the sampling of diffusion
models by parallelizing the autoregressive process. Specifically, we
reformulate the sampling process as solving a system of triangular nonlinear
equations through fixed-point iteration. With this innovative formulation, we
explore several systematic techniques to further reduce the iteration steps
required by the solving process. Applying these techniques, we introduce
ParaTAA, a universal and training-free parallel sampling algorithm that can
leverage extra computational and memory resources to increase the sampling
speed. Our experiments demonstrate that ParaTAA can decrease the inference
steps required by common sequential sampling algorithms such as DDIM and DDPM
by a factor of 4$\sim$14 times. Notably, when applying ParaTAA with 100 steps
DDIM for Stable Diffusion, a widely-used text-to-image diffusion model, it can
produce the same images as the sequential sampling in only 7 inference steps.
The code is available at https://github.com/TZW1998/ParaTAA-Diffusion.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses the problem of time-consuming sampling from diffusion models, a state-of-the-art in image generation. The key contribution is the introduction of ParaTAA, a novel parallel sampling algorithm that significantly accelerates the sampling process by parallelizing the autoregressive nature of existing methods, achieving a reduction in inference steps by a factor of 4 to 14 while maintaining image quality.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves reformulating the sampling process of diffusion models as solving triangular nonlinear equations with fixed-point iteration. Several techniques are integrated to enhance sampling efficiency, including an improved iteration function, a tailored Anderson Acceleration for triangular equations, and strategies for early stopping and initialization from previous trajectories.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using common diffusion models like DDIM and DDPM across various sampling scenarios with steps ranging from 25 to 100. The evaluation of model performance used metrics such as FID, Inception Score, and CLIP Score on a range of samples generated to assess image quality and convergence speed.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The limitations highlighted include the need for considerable computational resources, specifically multiple GPUs, to perform effectively, and the necessity of setting hyperparameters such as the history size and window size, which may impact convergence and performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may explore the application of the proposed methods to other autoregressive processes, including autoregressive video generative models, and further theoretical investigations into the convergence properties of the triangular Anderson acceleration approach.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), which combines Wasserstein auto-encoder and Wasserstein GAN to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds, enhancing data generation quality and representation learning.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The LWGAN framework modifies the latent distribution to a generalized normal distribution, introduces a deterministic encoder and a generator, and employs the 1-Wasserstein distance for measuring similarities between distributions. The model includes theoretical guarantees for consistency and generalization error bounds.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model is tested on synthetic datasets (such as Swiss roll, S-curve, Hyperplane) and benchmark datasets (MNIST, CelebA), using various neural network architectures for encoder, generator, and critic. Validation includes assessing intrinsic dimension detection and the quality of generated samples.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The approach assumes that the intrinsic dimension is known or can be accurately estimated, which may not always be possible. Additionally, the performance heavily relies on the selection of model parameters and the potential for mode collapse exists if the latent space is not correctly specified.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore scenarios where the generator is stochastic, integrate LWGAN with modern GAN architectures for improved generation capabilities, and apply LWGAN to structural estimation in fields like economics.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Score identity Distillation: Exponentially Fast Distillation of
  Pretrained Diffusion Models for One-Step Generation
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Score identity Distillation (SiD), an innovative data-free
method that distills the generative capabilities of pretrained diffusion models
into a single-step generator. SiD not only facilitates an exponentially fast
reduction in Fr\'echet inception distance (FID) during distillation but also
approaches or even exceeds the FID performance of the original teacher
diffusion models. By reformulating forward diffusion processes as semi-implicit
distributions, we leverage three score-related identities to create an
innovative loss mechanism. This mechanism achieves rapid FID reduction by
training the generator using its own synthesized images, eliminating the need
for real data or reverse-diffusion-based generation, all accomplished within
significantly shortened generation time. Upon evaluation across four benchmark
datasets, the SiD algorithm demonstrates high iteration efficiency during
distillation and surpasses competing distillation approaches, whether they are
one-step or few-step, data-free, or dependent on training data, in terms of
generation quality. This achievement not only redefines the benchmarks for
efficiency and effectiveness in diffusion distillation but also in the broader
field of diffusion-based generation. The PyTorch implementation is available at
https://github.com/mingyuanzhou/SiD
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents Score identity Distillation (SiD), a novel method for distilling generative capabilities from pretrained diffusion models into a single-step generator. SiD significantly reduces Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) during the distillation process, achieving performance that approaches or even exceeds that of the original diffusion models.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology is based on reformulating forward diffusion processes as semi-implicit distributions and utilizing three score-related identities to create a loss mechanism. The generator is trained using its own synthesized images via a model-based score-matching loss, minimizing discrepancies between scores of real and generated data distributions.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involved four benchmark datasets: CIFAR-10, ImageNet, FFHQ, and AFHQv2, utilizing established evaluation protocols such as FID and Inception Score for performance measurement. The SiD generator was trained with a batch size of 256-8192 and varied parameters across experiments.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method requires management of three networks (the pretrained score network, generator score network, and generator) during training, resulting in higher memory usage compared to traditional methods. Additionally, the increase in computational times due to score gradient calculations may affect efficiency.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may explore reducing memory requirements through techniques like LoRA, and investigate more efficient parameter scheduling to improve training times and model performance. Further development could also involve applying SiD in various generative contexts beyond image synthesis.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Sequential Neural Score Estimation: Likelihood-Free Inference with
  Conditional Score Based Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a
score-based method for Bayesian inference in simulator-based models. Our
method, inspired by the remarkable success of score-based methods in generative
modelling, leverages conditional score-based diffusion models to generate
samples from the posterior distribution of interest. The model is trained using
an objective function which directly estimates the score of the posterior. We
embed the model into a sequential training procedure, which guides simulations
using the current approximation of the posterior at the observation of
interest, thereby reducing the simulation cost. We also introduce several
alternative sequential approaches, and discuss their relative merits. We then
validate our method, as well as its amortised, non-sequential, variant on
several numerical examples, demonstrating comparable or superior performance to
existing state-of-the-art methods such as Sequential Neural Posterior
Estimation (SNPE).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a method for Bayesian inference using conditional score-based diffusion models to sample from posterior distributions where the likelihood is intractable.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology employs score matching techniques to estimate the score of the posterior and utilizes a sequential training approach to improve efficiency by guiding simulations toward informative regions of the parameter space.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is validated on various numerical benchmarks including SLCP and Lotka Volterra, and a real-world problem in neuroscience. The benchmarks include a uniform prior and simulations for inference. The performance is compared with existing methods such as Sequential Neural Posterior Estimation (SNPE).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: A key limitation is the computational cost associated with training and evaluating the model, particularly the need for many forward passes to compute likelihoods. The performance of certain variants may also degrade due to high variance in updates, especially in methods that rely on importance weights.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work includes investigating sequential variants of other simulation-based inference methods and exploring advanced neural network architectures for improved performance.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper presents Purify++, a new diffusion purification algorithm that enhances adversarial robustness in neural networks by systematically addressing limitations in previous diffusion purification methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance against multiple adversarial attacks.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ incorporates an improved diffusion model using variance exploding diffusion, efficient numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness to enhance adversarial purification processes.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilized datasets such as CIFAR10 and MNIST, evaluating performance against various black-box and gray-box attack methods (e.g., PGD, Square Attack) and measuring both standard and robust accuracies across different classifier architectures.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: Key limitations include the computational demands of higher-order numerical simulation methods and the need for optimal tuning of randomness strength, which may vary across different attack scenarios.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research may focus on further enhancing diffusion models, exploring new purification algorithms that integrate additional defensive strategies, and conducting broader evaluations against emerging adversarial attack methods.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Slicedit: Zero-Shot Video Editing With Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
  Using Spatio-Temporal Slices
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art results in
image synthesis and editing. However, leveraging such pretrained models for
video editing is considered a major challenge. Many existing works attempt to
enforce temporal consistency in the edited video through explicit
correspondence mechanisms, either in pixel space or between deep features.
These methods, however, struggle with strong nonrigid motion. In this paper, we
introduce a fundamentally different approach, which is based on the observation
that spatiotemporal slices of natural videos exhibit similar characteristics to
natural images. Thus, the same T2I diffusion model that is normally used only
as a prior on video frames, can also serve as a strong prior for enhancing
temporal consistency by applying it on spatiotemporal slices. Based on this
observation, we present Slicedit, a method for text-based video editing that
utilizes a pretrained T2I diffusion model to process both spatial and
spatiotemporal slices. Our method generates videos that retain the structure
and motion of the original video while adhering to the target text. Through
extensive experiments, we demonstrate Slicedit's ability to edit a wide range
of real-world videos, confirming its clear advantages compared to existing
competing methods. Webpage: https://matankleiner.github.io/slicedit/
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces Slicedit, a zero-shot video editing method which leverages pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models to edit videos while maintaining spatial and temporal consistency, especially in scenarios with complex motion and occlusions.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: Slicedit employs a novel approach that utilizes spatiotemporal slices and an inflated denoiser integrated with extended attention mechanisms. This method applies a text-to-image denoiser to both spatial and temporal slices of video data, allowing for simultaneous editing across both dimensions without fine-tuning the model.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments were performed on a dataset of 60 real-world video clips collected from the DA VIS dataset and the LOVEU-TGVE dataset, featuring various types of motion. The evaluation involved metrics for editing fidelity, structure preservation, and temporal consistency compared to existing methods, supplemented by user studies for qualitative assessment.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method is primarily focused on preserving video structure and may struggle with global editing tasks that require altering the entire context of a scene. Additionally, it is limited in cases of major structural changes, such as transforming one type of object into another unrelated type, like a dog into an elephant.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore improving global editing capabilities, enhancing the algorithm's robustness in extreme motion scenarios, and potentially integrating additional modalities like audio to create a more holistic editing experience.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
NaturalSpeech 3: Zero-Shot Speech Synthesis with Factorized Codec and
  Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
While recent large-scale text-to-speech (TTS) models have achieved
significant progress, they still fall short in speech quality, similarity, and
prosody. Considering speech intricately encompasses various attributes (e.g.,
content, prosody, timbre, and acoustic details) that pose significant
challenges for generation, a natural idea is to factorize speech into
individual subspaces representing different attributes and generate them
individually. Motivated by it, we propose NaturalSpeech 3, a TTS system with
novel factorized diffusion models to generate natural speech in a zero-shot
way. Specifically, 1) we design a neural codec with factorized vector
quantization (FVQ) to disentangle speech waveform into subspaces of content,
prosody, timbre, and acoustic details; 2) we propose a factorized diffusion
model to generate attributes in each subspace following its corresponding
prompt. With this factorization design, NaturalSpeech 3 can effectively and
efficiently model intricate speech with disentangled subspaces in a
divide-and-conquer way. Experiments show that NaturalSpeech 3 outperforms the
state-of-the-art TTS systems on quality, similarity, prosody, and
intelligibility, and achieves on-par quality with human recordings.
Furthermore, we achieve better performance by scaling to 1B parameters and 200K
hours of training data.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses significant limitations in text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis systems, particularly zero-shot synthesis, by introducing NaturalSpeech 3, which employs factorized diffusion models for improved speech quality, similarity, prosody, and intelligibility. It factorizes speech into subspaces representing different attributes and generates them independently, achieving human-level quality and surpassing state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: NaturalSpeech 3 uses a novel neural codec called FACodec with factorized vector quantization (FVQ) to decompose speech into distinct subspaces (content, prosody, timbre, acoustic details). It further employs a factorized diffusion model to generate each attribute based on corresponding prompts, allowing for better control and quality in speech synthesis.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The system was trained using the Librilight dataset, containing 60K hours of unlabeled speech data from diverse speakers. It was evaluated using benchmarks like LibriSpeech for TTS quality and RA VDESS for prosody evaluation, measuring metrics such as similarity, intelligibility, and quality (e.g., CMOS, SMOS, WER).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The current TTS system focuses on attributes like content and prosody, which does not encompass all aspects of speech (e.g., background sounds), limits scalability due to reliance on phoneme transcription, and lacks multilingual capabilities as it was trained solely on English audio. Additionally, the system's performance relies heavily on extensive amounts of training data, which may restrict its applicability in resource-scarce scenarios.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work can explore additional speech attributes like energy and background sounds, enhance multilingual support by diversifying training datasets, advance the neural codec for improved performance without phoneme dependency, and apply the FACodec model to other tasks beyond TTS, including automatic speech recognition and voice conversion.

selected_base_paper_info.title: Accelerating Parallel Sampling of Diffusion Models
selected_base_paper_info.summary:
Diffusion models have emerged as state-of-the-art generative models for image
generation. However, sampling from diffusion models is usually time-consuming
due to the inherent autoregressive nature of their sampling process. In this
work, we propose a novel approach that accelerates the sampling of diffusion
models by parallelizing the autoregressive process. Specifically, we
reformulate the sampling process as solving a system of triangular nonlinear
equations through fixed-point iteration. With this innovative formulation, we
explore several systematic techniques to further reduce the iteration steps
required by the solving process. Applying these techniques, we introduce
ParaTAA, a universal and training-free parallel sampling algorithm that can
leverage extra computational and memory resources to increase the sampling
speed. Our experiments demonstrate that ParaTAA can decrease the inference
steps required by common sequential sampling algorithms such as DDIM and DDPM
by a factor of 4$\sim$14 times. Notably, when applying ParaTAA with 100 steps
DDIM for Stable Diffusion, a widely-used text-to-image diffusion model, it can
produce the same images as the sequential sampling in only 7 inference steps.
The code is available at https://github.com/TZW1998/ParaTAA-Diffusion.
selected_base_paper_info.main_contributions: The research addresses the problem of time-consuming sampling from diffusion models, a state-of-the-art in image generation. The key contribution is the introduction of ParaTAA, a novel parallel sampling algorithm that significantly accelerates the sampling process by parallelizing the autoregressive nature of existing methods, achieving a reduction in inference steps by a factor of 4 to 14 while maintaining image quality.
selected_base_paper_info.methodology: The methodology involves reformulating the sampling process of diffusion models as solving triangular nonlinear equations with fixed-point iteration. Several techniques are integrated to enhance sampling efficiency, including an improved iteration function, a tailored Anderson Acceleration for triangular equations, and strategies for early stopping and initialization from previous trajectories.
selected_base_paper_info.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using common diffusion models like DDIM and DDPM across various sampling scenarios with steps ranging from 25 to 100. The evaluation of model performance used metrics such as FID, Inception Score, and CLIP Score on a range of samples generated to assess image quality and convergence speed.
selected_base_paper_info.limitations: The limitations highlighted include the need for considerable computational resources, specifically multiple GPUs, to perform effectively, and the necessity of setting hyperparameters such as the history size and window size, which may impact convergence and performance.
selected_base_paper_info.future_research_directions: Future research may explore the application of the proposed methods to other autoregressive processes, including autoregressive video generative models, and further theoretical investigations into the convergence properties of the triangular Anderson acceleration approach.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Sequential Neural Score Estimation: Likelihood-Free Inference with
  Conditional Score Based Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
We introduce Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a
score-based method for Bayesian inference in simulator-based models. Our
method, inspired by the remarkable success of score-based methods in generative
modelling, leverages conditional score-based diffusion models to generate
samples from the posterior distribution of interest. The model is trained using
an objective function which directly estimates the score of the posterior. We
embed the model into a sequential training procedure, which guides simulations
using the current approximation of the posterior at the observation of
interest, thereby reducing the simulation cost. We also introduce several
alternative sequential approaches, and discuss their relative merits. We then
validate our method, as well as its amortised, non-sequential, variant on
several numerical examples, demonstrating comparable or superior performance to
existing state-of-the-art methods such as Sequential Neural Posterior
Estimation (SNPE).
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents Sequential Neural Posterior Score Estimation (SNPSE), a novel method for Bayesian inference in simulator-based models using score-based diffusion models to estimate posterior distributions. The method reduces simulation costs by sequentially training models guided by approximations of the posterior.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: SNPSE employs conditional score-based diffusion models and a sequential training approach to improve efficiency in sampling from posteriors. It calculates scores using neural networks and includes alternative sequential approaches like Truncated SNPSE (TSNPSE), which utilizes truncated proposal priors to enhance performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The authors benchmarked SNPSE and its non-sequential variant (NPSE) against established methods like SNPE across various numerical examples and a real-world neuroscience problem, utilizing simulation budgets of 1000, 10000, and 100000 samples, and employing standard statistical measures such as the C2ST score.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The main limitation concerns the computational cost associated with TSNPSE, particularly in estimating the highest probability region of the posterior to define proposals, which requires multiple function evaluations and can be expensive compared to methods using normalising flows.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore the application of sequential methods to other models, develop more complex score networks, and investigate the use of energy-based parameterisations of diffusion models to reduce computational costs while maintaining sample quality.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Bridging discrete and continuous state spaces: Exploring the Ehrenfest
  process in time-continuous diffusion models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative modeling via stochastic processes has led to remarkable empirical
results as well as to recent advances in their theoretical understanding. In
principle, both space and time of the processes can be discrete or continuous.
In this work, we study time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state
spaces and investigate their correspondence to state-continuous diffusion
processes given by SDEs. In particular, we revisit the $\textit{Ehrenfest
process}$, which converges to an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process in the infinite
state space limit. Likewise, we can show that the time-reversal of the
Ehrenfest process converges to the time-reversed Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.
This observation bridges discrete and continuous state spaces and allows to
carry over methods from one to the respective other setting. Additionally, we
suggest an algorithm for training the time-reversal of Markov jump processes
which relies on conditional expectations and can thus be directly related to
denoising score matching. We demonstrate our methods in multiple convincing
numerical experiments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper investigates the relationship between time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state spaces and state-continuous diffusion processes, particularly focusing on the Ehrenfest process. It proposes a new loss function for training these models and establishes a correspondence to score-based generative models, allowing for better performance by leveraging insights from both discrete and continuous settings.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors introduce an algorithm for training the time-reversal of Markov jump processes based on conditional expectations, which connects directly to denoising score matching. They also analyze the Ehrenfest process, derive its time-reversed jump moments, and show their correspondence to continuous-state generative modeling.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The study includes numerical experiments on various datasets, including illustrative examples, MNIST, and CIFAR-10. The methods were validated through comparative performance metrics like Inception Score and Frechet Inception Distance, alongside visual assessments of generated outputs from different loss functions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper acknowledges the reliance on specific approximations for learning conditional expectations, and potential challenges in sampling from large state spaces, particularly concerning the jump rates and numerical stability when simulating time-reversed processes.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may explore generalizing the convergence of time-reversed jump processes to other birth-death processes, enhance transfer learning capabilities between discrete and continuous models, and investigate optimizations for improving numerical stability and model performance.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
TERD: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Diffusion Models Against
  Backdoors
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have achieved notable success in image generation, but they
remain highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, which compromise their integrity
by producing specific undesirable outputs when presented with a pre-defined
trigger. In this paper, we investigate how to protect diffusion models from
this dangerous threat. Specifically, we propose TERD, a backdoor defense
framework that builds unified modeling for current attacks, which enables us to
derive an accessible reversed loss. A trigger reversion strategy is further
employed: an initial approximation of the trigger through noise sampled from a
prior distribution, followed by refinement through differential multi-step
samplers. Additionally, with the reversed trigger, we propose backdoor
detection from the noise space, introducing the first backdoor input detection
approach for diffusion models and a novel model detection algorithm that
calculates the KL divergence between reversed and benign distributions.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that TERD secures a 100% True Positive Rate
(TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) across datasets of varying resolutions. TERD
also demonstrates nice adaptability to other Stochastic Differential Equation
(SDE)-based models. Our code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ML/TERD.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents TERD, a defense framework for diffusion models against backdoor attacks, offering a unified modeling of existing attacks and introducing an accessible reversed loss to enhance trigger reversion and detection capabilities, achieving a 100% True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate across various datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology includes a two-stage trigger reversion process: initial approximation of the trigger using prior noise followed by refinement through differential multi-step samplers. It also introduces new detection approaches based on KL divergence for identifying backdoor inputs and models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset, with evaluations extending to CelebA and CelebA-HQ datasets. The performance metrics included True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate for both input and model detection, with a focus on various backdoor attack types including BadDiffusion, TrojDiff, and VillanDiffusion.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations, but acknowledges challenges with trigger estimation under high variance attacks and complexity in optimizing multiple steps of the denoising process.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential future research directions include improving the scalability of the defense to different model architectures and further investigating methods to enhance the robustness of diffusion models against adaptive backdoor attacks.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: DiffDA: a Diffusion Model for Weather-scale Data Assimilation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
The generation of initial conditions via accurate data assimilation is
crucial for weather forecasting and climate modeling. We propose DiffDA as a
denoising diffusion model capable of assimilating atmospheric variables using
predicted states and sparse observations. Acknowledging the similarity between
a weather forecast model and a denoising diffusion model dedicated to weather
applications, we adapt the pretrained GraphCast neural network as the backbone
of the diffusion model. Through experiments based on simulated observations
from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, our method can produce assimilated global
atmospheric data consistent with observations at 0.25 deg (~30km) resolution
globally. This marks the highest resolution achieved by ML data assimilation
models. The experiments also show that the initial conditions assimilated from
sparse observations (less than 0.96% of gridded data) and 48-hour forecast can
be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents DiffDA, a novel machine learning method for data assimilation suitable for high-resolution weather-scale atmospheric variables (0.25°), achieving consistent results with less than 0.96% of the total data from sparse observations. It enables the generation of assimilated atmospheric data ready for weather forecasting applications, significantly enhancing operational efficiency in weather prediction.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffDA utilizes a denoising diffusion probabilistic model adapted from the pretrained GraphCast model as its backbone for atmospheric data assimilation. The model conditions on both predicted states and sparse observations during inference, employing techniques from in-painting and soft masking to improve conditioning accuracy.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted using simulated observations from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, with model inputs being atmospheric states and observations at a horizontal resolution of 0.25° and 13 vertical levels. The model's effectiveness was validated through comparisons of RMSEs against ground truth data across various setups and scenarios, testing both single-step and autoregressive data assimilation methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: Current limitations include the inability to incorporate multiple types of observations such as satellite imagery or radar soundings and reliance on simulated observations for quality control. The model's training data only includes point measurements from a single time step, which constrains its application in more dynamic forecasting environments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work will explore integrating broader types of observations (including satellite imagery), enhancing quality control methods within the data assimilation process, and developing capabilities for multi-time-step observations to strengthen the model's output stability and accuracy.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents Purify++, an enhanced diffusion purification algorithm that improves the defense against adversarial attacks using diffusion models. It explores and improves upon three key aspects: an advanced diffusion model, efficient numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance on multiple adversarial attacks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ utilizes an improved diffusion model based on variance exploding (VE) diffusion, incorporates advanced sampling techniques for purification, and employs optimal stochastic control to manage randomness during purification. It leverages numerical simulation methods, specifically Heun's method for efficient computation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Extensive experiments were conducted using datasets like CIFAR10 and MNIST to evaluate the robustness of the proposed method against various adversarial attacks, including black-box and gray-box attacks. Metrics used for evaluation include standard accuracy and robust accuracy on adversarial examples.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper does not discuss specific limitations in detail, but it implies assumptions regarding the conditions under which diffusion purification is effective. The robustness may vary with different types of adversarial attacks that were not explored comprehensively.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on further extending diffusion purification methods, exploring more advanced randomness control techniques, and applying Purify++ to different domains beyond image classification to assess its generalization capabilities.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Isometric Representation Learning for Disentangled Latent Space of
  Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
The latent space of diffusion model mostly still remains unexplored, despite
its great success and potential in the field of generative modeling. In fact,
the latent space of existing diffusion models are entangled, with a distorted
mapping from its latent space to image space. To tackle this problem, we
present Isometric Diffusion, equipping a diffusion model with a geometric
regularizer to guide the model to learn a geometrically sound latent space of
the training data manifold. This approach allows diffusion models to learn a
more disentangled latent space, which enables smoother interpolation, more
accurate inversion, and more precise control over attributes directly in the
latent space. Our extensive experiments consisting of image interpolations,
image inversions, and linear editing show the effectiveness of our method.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper addresses the challenge of entangled latent spaces in diffusion models by proposing Isometric Diffusion, which introduces a geometric regularizer to achieve a more disentangled latent space. Key contributions include enabling smoother interpolation, accurate inversion, and precise control over image attributes by learning an isometric representation of the latent space.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The authors utilize isometric representation learning, applying a novel loss function to guide the learning of an isometric mapping from the latent space to the data manifold, effectively making the latent space reflect the underlying geometry of the data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted on CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Church, and LSUN-Bedrooms datasets. They employed metrics such as Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Perceptual Path Length (PPL), and Linear Separability (LS) to evaluate the performance of their method against standard benchmarks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method introduces a potential trade-off between image quality (FID) and disentanglement metrics (PPL), which could be critical depending on the application. Additionally, there might be computational constraints due to the iterative nature of diffusion models affecting real-time applicability.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore the application of Isometric Diffusion in conditional generation scenarios and its integration with larger models such as text-to-image systems, along with a deeper examination of minimizing the trade-off between image quality and disentanglement.

The evaluation of model performance used metrics such as FID, Inception Score, and CLIP Score on a range of samples generated to assess image quality and convergence speed.","limitations":"The limitations highlighted include the need for considerable computational resources, specifically multiple GPUs, to perform effectively, and the necessity of setting hyperparameters such as the history size and window size, which may impact convergence and performance.","future_research_directions":"Future research may explore the application of the proposed methods to other autoregressive processes, including autoregressive video generative models, and further theoretical investigations into the convergence properties of the triangular Anderson acceleration approach."}

{"arxiv_id":"2405.03549v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03549v1","title":"Bridging discrete and continuous state spaces: Exploring the Ehrenfest
  process in time-continuous diffusion models","authors":["Ludwig Winkler","Lorenz Richter","Manfred Opper"],"published_date":"2024-05-06T15:12:51Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Generative modeling via stochastic processes has led to remarkable empirical
results as well as to recent advances in their theoretical understanding. In
principle, both space and time of the processes can be discrete or continuous.
In this work, we study time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state
spaces and investigate their correspondence to state-continuous diffusion
processes given by SDEs. In particular, we revisit the $\textit{Ehrenfest
process}$, which converges to an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process in the infinite
state space limit. Likewise, we can show that the time-reversal of the
Ehrenfest process converges to the time-reversed Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.
This observation bridges discrete and continuous state spaces and allows to
carry over methods from one to the respective other setting. Additionally, we
suggest an algorithm for training the time-reversal of Markov jump processes
which relies on conditional expectations and can thus be directly related to
denoising score matching. We demonstrate our methods in multiple convincing
numerical experiments.","github_url":"https://github.com/w86763777/pytorch-ddpm","main_contributions":"The paper investigates the relationship between time-continuous Markov jump processes on discrete state spaces and state-continuous diffusion processes, particularly focusing on the Ehrenfest process. It proposes a new loss function for training these models and establishes a correspondence to score-based generative models, allowing for better performance by leveraging insights from both discrete and continuous settings.","methodology":"The authors introduce an algorithm for training the time-reversal of Markov jump processes based on conditional expectations, which connects directly to denoising score matching. They also analyze the Ehrenfest process, derive its time-reversed jump moments, and show their correspondence to continuous-state generative modeling.","experimental_setup":"The study includes numerical experiments on various datasets, including illustrative examples, MNIST, and CIFAR-10. The methods were validated through comparative performance metrics like Inception Score and Frechet Inception Distance, alongside visual assessments of generated outputs from different loss functions.","limitations":"The paper acknowledges the reliance on specific approximations for learning conditional expectations, and potential challenges in sampling from large state spaces, particularly concerning the jump rates and numerical stability when simulating time-reversed processes.","future_research_directions":"Future work may explore generalizing the convergence of time-reversed jump processes to other birth-death processes, enhance transfer learning capabilities between discrete and continuous models, and investigate optimizations for improving numerical stability and model performance."}

{"arxiv_id":"2409.05294v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2409.05294v1","title":"TERD: A Unified Framework for Safeguarding Diffusion Models Against
  Backdoors","authors":["Yichuan Mo","Hui Huang","Mingjie Li","Ang Li","Yisen Wang"],"published_date":"2024-09-09T03:02:16Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Diffusion models have achieved notable success in image generation, but they
remain highly vulnerable to backdoor attacks, which compromise their integrity
by producing specific undesirable outputs when presented with a pre-defined
trigger. In this paper, we investigate how to protect diffusion models from
this dangerous threat. Specifically, we propose TERD, a backdoor defense
framework that builds unified modeling for current attacks, which enables us to
derive an accessible reversed loss. A trigger reversion strategy is further
employed: an initial approximation of the trigger through noise sampled from a
prior distribution, followed by refinement through differential multi-step
samplers. Additionally, with the reversed trigger, we propose backdoor
detection from the noise space, introducing the first backdoor input detection
approach for diffusion models and a novel model detection algorithm that
calculates the KL divergence between reversed and benign distributions.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that TERD secures a 100% True Positive Rate
(TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) across datasets of varying resolutions. TERD
also demonstrates nice adaptability to other Stochastic Differential Equation
(SDE)-based models. Our code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ML/TERD.","github_url":"https://github.com/PKU-ML/TERD","main_contributions":"The paper presents TERD, a defense framework for diffusion models against backdoor attacks, offering a unified modeling of existing attacks and introducing an accessible reversed loss to enhance trigger reversion and detection capabilities, achieving a 100% True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate across various datasets.","methodology":"The methodology includes a two-stage trigger reversion process: initial approximation of the trigger using prior noise followed by refinement through differential multi-step samplers. It also introduces new detection approaches based on KL divergence for identifying backdoor inputs and models.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset, with evaluations extending to CelebA and CelebA-HQ datasets. The performance metrics included True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate for both input and model detection, with a focus on various backdoor attack types including BadDiffusion, TrojDiff, and VillanDiffusion.","limitations":"The paper does not explicitly discuss limitations, but acknowledges challenges with trigger estimation under high variance attacks and complexity in optimizing multiple steps of the denoising process.","future_research_directions":"Potential future research directions include improving the scalability of the defense to different model architectures and further investigating methods to enhance the robustness of diffusion models against adaptive backdoor attacks."}

{"arxiv_id":"2401.05932v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05932v3","title":"DiffDA: a Diffusion Model for Weather-scale Data Assimilation","authors":["Langwen Huang","Lukas Gianinazzi","Yuejiang Yu","Peter D. Dueben","Torsten Hoefler"],"published_date":"2024-01-11T14:11:12Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"The generation of initial conditions via accurate data assimilation is
crucial for weather forecasting and climate modeling. We propose DiffDA as a
denoising diffusion model capable of assimilating atmospheric variables using
predicted states and sparse observations. Acknowledging the similarity between
a weather forecast model and a denoising diffusion model dedicated to weather
applications, we adapt the pretrained GraphCast neural network as the backbone
of the diffusion model. Through experiments based on simulated observations
from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, our method can produce assimilated global
atmospheric data consistent with observations at 0.25 deg (~30km) resolution
globally. This marks the highest resolution achieved by ML data assimilation
models. The experiments also show that the initial conditions assimilated from
sparse observations (less than 0.96% of gridded data) and 48-hour forecast can
be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.","github_url":"https://github.com/spcl/DiffDA","main_contributions":"The paper presents DiffDA, a novel machine learning method for data assimilation suitable for high-resolution weather-scale atmospheric variables (0.25°), achieving consistent results with less than 0.96% of the total data from sparse observations. It enables the generation of assimilated atmospheric data ready for weather forecasting applications, significantly enhancing operational efficiency in weather prediction.","methodology":"DiffDA utilizes a denoising diffusion probabilistic model adapted from the pretrained GraphCast model as its backbone for atmospheric data assimilation. The model conditions on both predicted states and sparse observations during inference, employing techniques from in-painting and soft masking to improve conditioning accuracy.","experimental_setup":"The experiments were conducted using simulated observations from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, with model inputs being atmospheric states and observations at a horizontal resolution of 0.25° and 13 vertical levels. The model's effectiveness was validated through comparisons of RMSEs against ground truth data across various setups and scenarios, testing both single-step and autoregressive data assimilation methods.","limitations":"Current limitations include the inability to incorporate multiple types of observations such as satellite imagery or radar soundings and reliance on simulated observations for quality control. The model's training data only includes point measurements from a single time step, which constrains its application in more dynamic forecasting environments.","future_research_directions":"Future work will explore integrating broader types of observations (including satellite imagery), enhancing quality control methods within the data assimilation process, and developing capabilities for multi-time-step observations to strengthen the model's output stability and accuracy."}

{"arxiv_id":"2310.18762v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2310.18762v1","title":"Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness","authors":["Boya Zhang","Weijian Luo","Zhihua Zhang"],"published_date":"2023-10-28T17:18:38Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.","github_url":"https://github.com/openai/improved-diffusion","main_contributions":"The paper presents Purify++, an enhanced diffusion purification algorithm that improves the defense against adversarial attacks using diffusion models. It explores and improves upon three key aspects: an advanced diffusion model, efficient numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance on multiple adversarial attacks.","methodology":"Purify++ utilizes an improved diffusion model based on variance exploding (VE) diffusion, incorporates advanced sampling techniques for purification, and employs optimal stochastic control to manage randomness during purification. It leverages numerical simulation methods, specifically Heun's method for efficient computation.","experimental_setup":"Extensive experiments were conducted using datasets like CIFAR10 and MNIST to evaluate the robustness of the proposed method against various adversarial attacks, including black-box and gray-box attacks. Metrics used for evaluation include standard accuracy and robust accuracy on adversarial examples.","limitations":"The paper does not discuss specific limitations in detail, but it implies assumptions regarding the conditions under which diffusion purification is effective. The robustness may vary with different types of adversarial attacks that were not explored comprehensively.","future_research_directions":"Future research could focus on further extending diffusion purification methods, exploring more advanced randomness control techniques, and applying Purify++ to different domains beyond image classification to assess its generalization capabilities."}

{"arxiv_id":"2407.11451v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2407.11451v1","title":"Isometric Representation Learning for Disentangled Latent Space of
  Diffusion Models","authors":["Jaehoon Hahm","Junho Lee","Sunghyun Kim","Joonseok Lee"],"published_date":"2024-07-16T07:36:01Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"The latent space of diffusion model mostly still remains unexplored, despite
its great success and potential in the field of generative modeling. In fact,
the latent space of existing diffusion models are entangled, with a distorted
mapping from its latent space to image space. To tackle this problem, we
present Isometric Diffusion, equipping a diffusion model with a geometric
regularizer to guide the model to learn a geometrically sound latent space of
the training data manifold. This approach allows diffusion models to learn a
more disentangled latent space, which enables smoother interpolation, more
accurate inversion, and more precise control over attributes directly in the
latent space. Our extensive experiments consisting of image interpolations,
image inversions, and linear editing show the effectiveness of our method.","github_url":"https://github.com/isno0907/isodiff","main_contributions":"This paper addresses the challenge of entangled latent spaces in diffusion models by proposing Isometric Diffusion, which introduces a geometric regularizer to achieve a more disentangled latent space. Key contributions include enabling smoother interpolation, accurate inversion, and precise control over image attributes by learning an isometric representation of the latent space.","methodology":"The authors utilize isometric representation learning, applying a novel loss function to guide the learning of an isometric mapping from the latent space to the data manifold, effectively making the latent space reflect the underlying geometry of the data.","experimental_setup":"The experiments were conducted on CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN-Church, and LSUN-Bedrooms datasets. They employed metrics such as Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Perceptual Path Length (PPL), and Linear Separability (LS) to evaluate the performance of their method against standard benchmarks.","limitations":"The method introduces a potential trade-off between image quality (FID) and disentanglement metrics (PPL), which could be critical depending on the application. Additionally, there might be computational constraints due to the iterative nature of diffusion models affecting real-time applicability.","future_research_directions":"Future work could explore the application of Isometric Diffusion in conditional generation scenarios and its integration with larger models such as text-to-image systems, along with a deeper examination of minimizing the trade-off between image quality and disentanglement."}
new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3—a genuinely new sampling framework that draws on both the Base Method’s fixed‐point parallel sampling process and the discrete‐continuous bridging and conditional expectation ideas from the Ehrenfest process approach:

Introducing Hybrid Ehrenfest Diffusion Acceleration (HEDA)

Overview:
HEDA reframes the diffusion model’s sampling into a two-phase process that leverages the strengths of parallel fixed-point iterations (as in ParaTAA) while “discretizing” the evolution via an Ehrenfest-inspired state representation. In doing so, HEDA stabilizes the iterative sampling, reduces hyperparameter sensitivity, and lowers the heavy computational cost required by the Base Method.

Key Components:
1. Discrete State Projection:  
 • Instead of operating entirely in the continuous state space as in conventional diffusion sampling, HEDA first “projects” continuous latent trajectories onto a discrete set of “jump states” using a procedure inspired by the Ehrenfest process.  
 • This discretization is achieved by defining a mapping between regions of the continuous space and discrete states, which then naturally allows the use of conditional expectation techniques to predict likely transitions between states.

2. Conditional Expectation–Guided Fixed-Point Iteration:  
 • In the second phase, the sampling process is reformulated as solving a system of equations defined over these discrete states.  
 • A modified fixed-point iteration incorporates conditional expectations calculated from the discrete jump dynamics; these expectations act as robust “proposals” for state updates, thereby making the iterations less sensitive to initialization errors and hyperparameter choices (e.g., window or history sizes).

3. Accelerated and Resource-Efficient Parallel Sampling:  
 • With the discrete state structure acting as a stabilizing scaffold, the parallel fixed-point iterations can converge in fewer steps—similar to ParaTAA’s acceleration—while reducing the computational overhead associated with fine-grained continuous updates.  
 • By safely bridging the discrete and continuous regimes, HEDA dispenses with overly sensitive hyperparameters and can be tuned to run effectively on more modest computational resource allocations.

Why HEDA Addresses Base Method’s Challenges:
• It mitigates the resource-intensive nature of high‐resolution fixed-point iterations by “chunking” the state space into discrete segments and leveraging conditional expectation to skip over redundant computations.
• The discrete projection regularizes the sampling trajectories, reducing the reliance on sensitive hyperparameter settings associated with convergence behavior in the Base Method’s purely continuous fixed-point iterations.
• By embedding Ehrenfest‐process insights, the method offers a principled way to estimate and control uncertainty in each iteration step, thereby enhancing numerical stability without sacrificing image fidelity.

In summary, Hybrid Ehrenfest Diffusion Acceleration (HEDA) is a truly novel approach that synthesizes the parallel fixed-point formulation from ParaTAA with a discrete, conditional-expectation framework inspired by the Ehrenfest process. This new method tackles key bottlenecks of computational resource demands and hyperparameter sensitivity, paving the way for faster, more stable, and resource-efficient diffusion sampling.

def taa_step(self, t, direction, residual, unfinished_index):
    # Determine which timesteps are finished
    if len(unfinished_index) == 0:
        self.finished_index = t[0]
    else:
        self.finished_index = t[unfinished_index.max()].item() 

    # Compute higher-order residual
    ho_residual = self.compute_ho_residual(t, direction)

    # Initialize or update memory for Anderson acceleration
    Gf = torch.zeros_like(ho_residual)
    if self.residual_memory is None:
        # First iteration initialization
        self.residual_memory = torch.zeros(1, self.num_steps + 1, 4, 64, 64, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float64)
        self.samples_memory = torch.zeros(1, self.num_steps + 1, 4, 64, 64, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float64)
        self.residual_memory[0, t] = ho_residual
        self.samples_memory[0, t] = self._samples[t]
        self.memory_indexes[t] = torch.clamp(self.memory_indexes[t] + 1, max=self.memory_size)
    else:
        # Update memory with new residuals and samples
        padded_residual = torch.zeros(1, self.num_steps + 1, 4, 64, 64, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float64)
        padded_samples = torch.zeros(1, self.num_steps + 1, 4, 64, 64, device=self.device, dtype=torch.float64)
        padded_residual[0, t] = ho_residual
        padded_samples[0, t] = self._samples[t]
        self.residual_memory = torch.cat([self.residual_memory, padded_residual], dim=0)
        self.samples_memory = torch.cat([self.samples_memory, padded_samples], dim=0)

        # Keep only the most recent memory_size iterations
        self.residual_memory = self.residual_memory[-self.memory_size:]
        self.samples_memory = self.samples_memory[-self.memory_size:]
        self.memory_indexes[t] = torch.clamp(self.memory_indexes[t] + 1, max=self.memory_size)

        # Compute differences between consecutive iterations
        residual_diff = self.residual_memory[1:] - self.residual_memory[:-1]
        sample_diff = self.samples_memory[1:] - self.samples_memory[:-1]
        
        # Apply Anderson acceleration
        if len(update_indexes) > 0:
            # Construct matrices for Anderson acceleration
            # [Matrix construction code...]
            
            # Solve linear system for Anderson coefficients
            if use_memory_max == 1:
                solve_d = d / B.squeeze(-1)
            else:
                solve_d = torch.linalg.solve(B, d) # [len(update_indexes), m_k]
            
            # Compute acceleration term
            A = sample_diff_mat[:,:len(update_indexes),:,:,:] + res_diff_mat[:,:len(update_indexes),:,:,:]
            Gf_flat = torch.einsum("ijklm,ji->jklm", A, solve_d)
            Gf[update_indexes] = Gf_flat
    
    # Update samples with acceleration
    self._samples[t] = self._samples[t] + ho_residual - Gf
ml_model.details: The repository implements two main models: DiT (Diffusion Transformer) and Stable Diffusion v1.5. The ParaTAA algorithm reformulates the sampling process of diffusion models as solving a system of triangular nonlinear equations through fixed-point iteration.
